close_reason: Acceptance criteria satisfied: FetchNIP65, NIP65SelfSync, and fetchKind10050 all route through shared metadata verification enforcing expected kind/author plus CheckID/VerifySignature; tests cover wrong-author, invalid-signature, invalid-ID, and wrong-kind rejection across the metadata verification path.
description:
Nostr audit finding NP-01.

Observed behavior:
- `internal/nostr/runtime/nip65.go:FetchNIP65()` selects the best `kind:10002` event from relay results without `CheckID()` / `VerifySignature()`.
- `internal/nostr/runtime/nip65.go:NIP65SelfSync()` decodes and applies self-sync `kind:10002` events without local verification.
- `internal/nostr/runtime/nip65.go:fetchKind10050()` accepts relay-returned `kind:10050` events without local verification.

Why this matters:
These events directly influence relay routing and startup publish suppression. In Nostr, relay filtering is not a trust boundary; clients must still verify event IDs, signatures, and expected authors before treating metadata as authoritative.

Runtime risk:
A malicious or buggy relay can poison relay routing, steer DM/control traffic, blackhole traffic, or suppress startup publication by forging relay metadata.

Expected direction:
Add local verification (`CheckID`, `VerifySignature`, expected author match) before decoding or applying metadata events in all fetch/self-sync paths.
notes:
Started implementation.

Changes made:
- Added shared metadata verification in internal/nostr/runtime/nip65.go for expected author, expected kind, valid event ID, and valid signature.
- Wired FetchNIP65(), NIP65SelfSync(), and fetchKind10050() through the verification helper.
- Added nip65 tests covering wrong-author, invalid-signature, invalid-ID, and wrong-kind rejection.

Validation:
- go test ./internal/nostr/runtime/...

Remaining follow-up:
- Decide whether to add broader integration coverage around the public ingestion entry points beyond helper-level tests.
title: Verify kind:10002 and kind:10050 metadata before using it for relay routing

acceptance_criteria:
- `NIP65SelfSync()` accumulates the best pre-EOSE event and applies it once after EOSE.
- Post-EOSE updates are accepted only when newer than current state by `(CreatedAt, EventID)`.
- Startup behavior does not silently keep stale local relays when authoritative remote metadata already exists.
- Tests cover pre-EOSE catch-up, post-EOSE live updates, and stale-vs-new ordering across multiple relays.
close_reason: Completed: NIP-65 self-sync is now EOSE-aware, replaceable-event ordered, and covered by focused runtime tests.
description:
Nostr audit finding NP-02.

Observed behavior:
- `internal/nostr/runtime/nip65.go:NIP65SelfSync()` tracks only `lastEventID`.
- Pre-EOSE events are not applied to runtime state; only post-EOSE updates trigger `OnRelayUpdate`.
- Live updates are accepted in arrival order instead of replaceable-event order.

Why this matters:
For replaceable metadata (`kind:10002`), the client should accumulate the best stored event during catch-up, mark backfill completion on EOSE, apply the authoritative startup state once, and then only accept newer live updates based on `(CreatedAt, EventID)` ordering.

Runtime risk:
The daemon can start with stale local relay state even when newer remote metadata already exists, and stale multi-relay deliveries can overwrite newer state after startup.

Expected direction:
Use an explicit catch-up/live boundary at EOSE and align ordering logic with the safer pattern already present in `relay_sets.go` and `capability.go`.
notes:
Implemented NIP-65 self-sync catch-up/live ordering fix.

Changes made:
- Replaced lastEventID-based self-sync with a replaceable-event state machine in internal/nostr/runtime/nip65.go.
- Added CreatedAt + EventID ordering helper for replaceable metadata.
- Added runNIP65SelfSyncLoop() to accumulate the best verified pre-EOSE event, apply it once at EOSE, then accept only newer live updates.
- Drained already-buffered pre-EOSE events before applying the startup snapshot at EOSE.
- Suppressed redundant callback churn for semantically identical relay-list republishes.
- Added tests covering pre-EOSE catch-up, stale live update rejection, same-timestamp EventID tie-breaking, buffered EOSE/event interleaving, and semantic no-op republishes.

Validation:
- go test ./internal/nostr/runtime/...
title: Make NIP-65 self-sync EOSE-aware and replaceable-event ordered

description:
Nostr audit findings NP-03 and NP-07.

Observed behavior:
- `internal/gateway/channels/relay_filter.go:subscribeLoop()` forwards raw relay events to application callbacks without `CheckID()` / `VerifySignature()`.
- The CLOSED error path formats `rc.Relay.URL` without guarding for a nil relay pointer.

Why this matters:
This channel is effectively a raw Nostr event ingress path. Without local verification, downstream automation can treat forged relay traffic as trusted input. CLOSED handling should also be defensive because relay context may be absent on closure.

Runtime risk:
Forged events can trigger application behavior, and nil relay contexts can panic the daemon during normal subscription shutdown/error flow.

Expected direction:
Verify events before forwarding them, and preserve CLOSED reasons without dereferencing nil relay state.
notes:

- verify inbound relay-filter events with CheckID() and VerifySignature() before dedupe/callback delivery
- route CLOSED formatting through a helper that preserves handled-auth suppression and guards nil relay context with an <unknown relay> fallback

description:
Nostr audit finding NP-04.

Observed behavior:
- `internal/nostr/runtime/control_bus.go:handleInbound()` marks the event seen, then applies caller throttling and request-age rejection before consulting cached replay for cacheable methods.

Why this matters:
Nostr delivery is at-least-once across relays/reconnects. Legitimate duplicate control requests should hit idempotent replay first, especially after relay delays, reconnects, or restarts.

Runtime risk:
A caller can resend a request that was already handled and receive `rate limited` or `expired` instead of the original result, weakening retry safety and replay semantics.

Expected direction:
Decode the call, normalize the method, and consult replay cache before applying freshness/throttle rejection for cacheable methods.
notes:
Implemented fix in internal/nostr/runtime/control_bus.go:
- decode and normalize the control method before throttle/expiry checks
- consult cached replay for cacheable methods before rate-limit, expiry, and future-skew rejection
- preserve existing safety gates for cache misses and non-cacheable methods

Added focused coverage in internal/nostr/runtime/control_bus_test.go for:
- cacheable duplicate replay winning over stale/throttled delivery
- cache lookup occurring before expiry rejection on cache misses
- non-cacheable methods skipping cached replay lookup
- request handler short-circuiting on replayed or rejected requests

Validation:
- go test ./internal/nostr/runtime/...
title: Check cached control responses before throttle and expiry in ControlRPCBus

description:
Nostr audit finding NP-05.

Observed behavior:
- `cmd/metiq/cli_admin.go:nostrControlClient.call()` subscribes for `kind:21121` responses and then waits only for a matching `EVENT` or context timeout.
- It does not attach `OnClosed`, and subscription termination/auth-policy failures on the response path are collapsed into a generic timeout.

Why this matters:
A Nostr control client should react to actual relay outcomes (`CLOSED`, exhausted relays, auth failure) rather than rely solely on timeout expiration.

Runtime risk:
Explicit relay refusal or auth/policy failure looks identical to a slow daemon, making retries ambiguous and degrading protocol observability.

Expected direction:
Plumb response-subscription closure and failure signals into the client wait path so it can fail early with protocol-relevant context.
notes:
Implemented fix in cmd/metiq/cli_admin.go:
- switched nostrControlClient to a narrow hub interface for deterministic response-path testing
- added response subscription error channel alongside response event channel
- aggregate unhandled CLOSED outcomes across response relays and fail early once all response relays are closed
- ignore handled-auth CLOSED outcomes so they do not masquerade as hard failures
- surface silent subscription termination via a new optional hub SubOpts.OnEnd callback
- preserve generic timeout only for true no-response cases

Updated internal/nostr/runtime/hub.go:
- added optional SubOpts.OnEnd callback invoked when the subscription event stream ends unexpectedly without caller cancellation

description:
Nostr audit finding NP-06.

Observed behavior:
- `internal/nostr/runtime/hub.go:SubOpts` exposes `OnEOSE` and comments promise EOSE-aware subscription handling.
- `NostrHub.Subscribe()` currently uses `SubscribeManyNotifyClosed()` and never invokes `OnEOSE`.

Why this matters:
EOSE is the protocol-native signal for catch-up completion. An API that advertises EOSE but never emits it encourages higher-level code to build timeout-driven workarounds.

Runtime risk:
Callers may assume the hub supports EOSE-aware sync when it does not, leading to hidden protocol mismatches.

Expected direction:
Either implement aggregated EOSE handling in the hub or remove `OnEOSE` from the public contract until it is real.
notes:
Chose the contract-tightening path in internal/nostr/runtime/hub.go:
- removed unsupported SubOpts.OnEOSE callback from the public API
- updated package and Subscribe comments to describe the actual runtime contract: relay CLOSED and subscription-end lifecycle signals, not aggregated EOSE
- clarified that callers needing explicit EOSE handling should use a pool method that exposes EOSE directly

Added hub API-shape coverage in internal/nostr/runtime/hub_test.go to assert:
- SubOpts does not expose unsupported OnEOSE
- SubOpts still exposes OnClosed and OnEnd lifecycle callbacks

title: Align NostrHub subscription API with actual EOSE behavior

description:

Nostr-native reliability depends on understanding actual relay and subscription behavior. Operators need visibility into whether failures are caused by invalid events, stale replaceables, auth challenges, relay policy closures, or genuine no-response conditions.

Expected direction:

description:
Recommendation anchor: MCPCFG-01 in docs/refactor/src-mcp-transfer-recommendations.md.

Replace swarmstr's current startup-only `extra.mcp` parsing with a real MCP config registry and resolution layer.

Why this bead exists:
- `src` treats MCP configuration as a resolved inventory, not a single raw map. It supports scoped config resolution, precedence, deduplication, and policy/trust filtering before connection (`src/services/mcp/config.ts:619-759`, `src/services/mcp/config.ts:1033-1252`).
- swarmstr currently parses one `extra.mcp` block in `internal/mcp/manager.go` and wires it directly into startup registration in `cmd/metiqd/main.go:1001-1085`.
- Without a resolved config inventory, swarmstr cannot cleanly add reconnect, trust policy, CLI management, dynamic sources, or project-vs-managed MCP behavior.

Scope:
- Introduce a resolved MCP server inventory API that can aggregate configured sources and expose stable server metadata to the runtime.
- Define precedence/merge rules appropriate for swarmstr's control/config architecture.
- Add dedup hooks so future dynamic/plugin/project sources do not spawn duplicate MCP connections.

Non-goals:
- Do not implement OAuth flows in this bead.
- Do not add generic resources/prompts surfaces yet.

description:
Recommendation anchor: MCPCONN-01 in docs/refactor/src-mcp-transfer-recommendations.md.

Upgrade swarmstr's external MCP manager from a startup-time connector into a stateful connection manager with explicit per-server states, reconnect behavior, and capability refresh.

Why this bead exists:
- `src` models MCP servers as connected, failed, needs-auth, pending, or disabled and provides reconnect helpers plus capability-aware refresh behavior (`src/services/mcp/types.ts:180-226`, `src/services/mcp/client.ts:595-764`, `src/services/mcp/client.ts:2130-2249`).
- swarmstr currently either connects a server during startup or logs the failure; it does not maintain a richer lifecycle model in `internal/mcp/manager.go`.
- Immediate reconnection/capability refresh support is required before auth, resource/prompt discovery, or operator-facing health management can feel reliable.

Scope:
- Add a per-server state model to the manager.
- Support explicit reconnect and post-reconnect capability refresh.
- Separate discovery state from one-time startup success/failure.

Non-goals:
- Do not implement OAuth token acquisition here.
- Do not build CLI commands yet.

- Do not require Claude.ai-specific connector behavior.

description:

Extend ACP task payloads so workers can inherit the relevant runtime contract from the caller: profile/tool restrictions, memory scope, and optional parent context metadata.

Scope:

- Keep the protocol Go-native and ACP-native.

Non-goals:

description:
Convert the autonomy audit into an implementation program that moves Swarmstr from a strong autonomous agent harness to a fully independent agent architecture.

This epic tracks the work needed to add:
- first-class goal/task/run models
- planning and decomposition
- verification and reviewer loops
- authority, autonomy modes, and governance
- budget enforcement
- workflow recovery and idempotency
- multi-agent task semantics over ACP/Nostr
- task-centric observability and auditability
- episodic/shared memory improvements for autonomous work
- learning and policy evolution

The intent is to preserve Swarmstr's current runtime strengths (tool execution, memory, approvals, observability, Nostr-native routing) while adding the missing control-plane and task-plane contracts required for full independence.

Expose the new task model through operator-facing and agent-facing APIs.

Scope:
- add methods to create tasks/goals, list/query by status, inspect dependencies, cancel tasks, and resume blocked work
- decide which methods are operator-only versus agent-available
- align HTTP control, Nostr control RPC, and any WS projections

Likely touchpoints:
- internal/gateway/methods/schema.go
- cmd/metiqd/main.go
- internal/policy/control.go
notes: Added task control/gateway methods tasks.create, tasks.get, tasks.list, tasks.cancel, and tasks.resume. Extended internal/gateway/methods/schema.go with strict request normalization, supported-method registration, and alias compatibility for task IDs and filters. Implemented durable task CRUD/query/cancel/resume handling in cmd/metiqd/main.go with legal lifecycle transitions, automatic task ID generation, and resume semantics that either re-queue the current run or create a new run attempt. Added request/schema coverage in internal/gateway/methods/schema_test.go and end-to-end control RPC lifecycle coverage in cmd/metiqd/main_test.go. Validation: go test ./internal/gateway/methods ./cmd/metiqd -run 'Test(Tasks|HandleControlRPCRequest_TaskLifecycleMethods)' && go test ./cmd/metiqd -run '^$'
title: Task model: add control and gateway methods for task CRUD, query, cancel, and resume

description:
Ensure the new task plane is connected to the current turn/session runtime.

Scope:

- avoid requiring transcript reconstruction to understand what happened

- ACP history / session spawn / run execution paths
notes: Implemented task/session/run/result linkage across local spawn and ACP delegation paths. Added session-store task linkage fields and helpers; propagated task/run/result metadata through ACP worker metadata cloning; created inherited ACP child-task synthesis/binding helpers; persisted canonical worker TaskSpec/TaskRun docs plus session result linkage; attached task/run/result data to ACP turn telemetry; and seeded spawned child sessions with parent task/run linkage. Added targeted coverage in internal/store/state/session_store_test.go, cmd/metiqd/acp_inheritance_test.go, and cmd/metiqd/main_test.go. Validation: go test ./internal/store/state -run 'TestSessionStore_(RecordTurn|LinkTaskAndRecordTaskResult)|TestTurnTelemetry_JSONShape' && go test ./cmd/metiqd -run 'Test(BuildInheritedACPTaskPayloadUsesRuntimeHintsAndContext|ACPWorkerTaskDocs_PersistTaskRunAndResultLinkage|HandleACPMessageAppliesInheritedRuntimeHints|ApplySessionsSpawn_InheritsParentTaskLinkage|ExecuteAgentRun_EmitsAndPersistsTurnTelemetry)' && go test ./internal/acp ./internal/store/state ./cmd/metiqd -run '^$'

description: The WhatsApp extension starts an embedded HTTP server per channel instance, defaults every instance to the same listen address (:8080), and binds a fixed /webhook path. Multiple WhatsApp channels in one process will conflict on listen/bind. Separately, Send() falls back to a mutable default_recipient that is seeded from the first inbound sender, which makes replies ambiguous in multi-user scenarios. OpenClaw handles channel/account routing with shared registry infrastructure instead of per-instance listeners. Scope: move WhatsApp webhook handling behind shared routing/registration, make instance identification explicit, and remove implicit recipient routing from Send() or make it session-targeted and tested.
title: WhatsApp channel plugin is not safe for multi-instance routing

description: Swarmstr marks discord, slack, and telegram as Threads-capable, and each implementation exposes SendInThread, but inbound messages do not populate sdk.InboundChannelMessage.ThreadID or ReplyToEventID. In practice that means the runtime cannot recover thread/reply context from inbound events, even though OpenClaw has explicit thread-context handling and tests for these transports (for example slack/threading tests, discord monitor thread handling, telegram thread/webhook tests). Scope: capture the upstream thread identifiers from each provider (Slack thread_ts, Discord thread channel / reply reference, Telegram reply_to_message_id or topic/thread metadata where available), wire them into InboundChannelMessage, and add regression tests for inbound threaded messages.
title: Channel thread metadata parity is missing for Discord/Slack/Telegram

description:
Recommendation anchor: LOOP-02a in docs/refactor/src-agent-platform-transfer-recommendations.md.

Wire metiq's existing tool-loop detector into the shared agentic loop so it is enforced in the same place as tool scheduling and execution.

Why this bead exists:
- The investigation found that metiq already has a strong loop-detection subsystem in `internal/agent/toolloop/detection.go`, but the selected shared loop path does not clearly show it being enforced inside `executeToolsParallel()`.
- src does not offer a stronger generic detector than metiq here, so this is a metiq-native hardening bead rather than a direct port.
- This bead prevents regressions as tool contracts and scheduling become richer.

Scope:
- Call detect/record hooks in the shared loop before and after execution.
- Make blocks or warnings visible to turn result/history handling.
- Keep the detector implementation itself mostly intact.

Non-goals:
- Do not redesign detector heuristics from scratch.
- Do not couple detector output to UI-specific behavior.

description:
Follow-up code review found two concrete correctness issues in the current working changes:
- internal/nostr/runtime/hub.go can fire OnEnd on caller-driven cancellation if the events channel closes before the select observes subCtx.Done().
- cmd/metiq/cli_admin.go computes a deduped responseRelaySet for closure accounting but still subscribes using raw responseRelays, allowing redundant subscriptions to duplicate/whitespace-variant relay URLs.

Expected fix:
- guard OnEnd so it only fires for unexpected subscription termination
- subscribe using the sanitized responseRelaySet
- add focused regression tests
